description: The study of vectors, vector spaces, linear maps, and their representation through matrices. Foundational for modern mathematics, data science, physics, and engineering.

concepts.name: Vector
concepts.description: An ordered tuple of numbers (a vector in ℝⁿ) or an abstract element of a vector space. In ℝⁿ, vectors represent magnitudes with direction; algebraically they are the fundamental objects of linear algebra.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.1; Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.1

concepts.name: Vector Addition
concepts.description: Component-wise addition: (a₁,…,aₙ)+(b₁,…,bₙ)=(a₁+b₁,…,aₙ+bₙ). Geometrically, the parallelogram law. Satisfies commutativity, associativity, identity (zero vector), and inverse (negation).

concepts.name: Scalar Multiplication
concepts.description: Multiplying a vector by a scalar c: c(a₁,…,aₙ)=(ca₁,…,caₙ). Stretches or compresses the vector; negation reverses direction. Together with addition gives the vector space structure.

concepts.name: Dot Product
concepts.description: a·b = ∑aᵢbᵢ = |a||b|cos θ. Algebraically: sum of products of corresponding components. Geometrically: |a||b|cos(angle). Zero iff vectors are orthogonal.

concepts.name: Vector Norm
concepts.description: |v| = √(v·v) = √(∑vᵢ²). More generally, the p-norm: |v|_p = (∑|vᵢ|^p)^{1/p}. Satisfies positivity, homogeneity, and triangle inequality (defines a metric).

concepts.name: Unit Vector
concepts.description: A vector of norm 1. Obtained by normalizing: v̂ = v/|v|. Standard basis vectors e₁,…,eₙ are the canonical unit vectors in ℝⁿ.

concepts.name: Orthogonality
concepts.description: Two vectors are orthogonal iff their dot product is zero. Orthogonality generalizes perpendicularity to arbitrary inner product spaces. Orthogonal vectors are linearly independent.

concepts.name: Cross Product
concepts.description: Defined in ℝ³: a×b = (a₂b₃−a₃b₂, a₃b₁−a₁b₃, a₁b₂−a₂b₁). Result is perpendicular to both a and b; magnitude is |a||b|sin θ (area of parallelogram). Anti-commutative.

concepts.name: Matrix
concepts.description: A rectangular array of numbers with m rows and n columns. Encodes linear transformations (m×n matrix ↔ linear map ℝⁿ→ℝᵐ), system coefficients, data, and more.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.2

concepts.name: Matrix Addition
concepts.description: Component-wise addition of matrices of the same dimensions. Satisfies all vector space axioms — matrices of the same size form a vector space.

concepts.name: Matrix Multiplication
concepts.description: (AB)_{ij} = ∑_k A_{ik}B_{kj}; requires inner dimensions to match. Non-commutative in general. Associative; distributes over addition. Encodes composition of linear transformations.

concepts.name: Matrix Transpose
concepts.description: (Aᵀ)_{ij} = A_{ji}: rows become columns and vice versa. Key properties: (AB)ᵀ=BᵀAᵀ, (Aᵀ)ᵀ=A. Symmetric matrices satisfy A=Aᵀ.

concepts.name: Symmetric Matrix
concepts.description: A = Aᵀ. Symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors (Spectral Theorem). Ubiquitous in physics, statistics (covariance matrices), and optimization (Hessians).

concepts.name: System of Linear Equations
concepts.description: A set of m linear equations in n unknowns, written Ax=b. Three outcomes: unique solution (consistent, full column rank), no solution (inconsistent), or infinitely many solutions (dependent). Structure is determined by rank of A and [A|b].
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.2

concepts.name: Augmented Matrix
concepts.description: The matrix [A|b] formed by appending the right-hand side column b to the coefficient matrix A. Row operations on [A|b] preserve the solution set and are used to row-reduce to echelon form.

concepts.name: Row Reduction
concepts.description: Three elementary row operations (swap, scale, add multiple of one row to another) transform Ax=b without changing its solution set. Systematic application yields row echelon or reduced row echelon form.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, §2.2; Lay, Linear Algebra, §1.2

concepts.name: Row Echelon Form
concepts.description: REF: zeros below each pivot; RREF: additionally, zeros above each pivot and each pivot equals 1. RREF is unique. Pivot columns correspond to basic variables; non-pivot columns to free variables.
references: Lay, Linear Algebra and Its Applications, §1.2

concepts.name: Rank
concepts.description: The number of pivots in REF; equals the dimension of the column space (column rank) and row space (row rank). Column rank = row rank always. Rank determines existence and uniqueness of solutions to Ax=b.

concepts.name: Matrix Inverse
concepts.description: A square matrix A is invertible iff det(A)≠0, iff rank(A)=n, iff columns are linearly independent. The inverse A⁻¹ satisfies AA⁻¹=A⁻¹A=I. Computed via [A|I] → [I|A⁻¹] by row reduction.

concepts.name: Determinant
concepts.description: A scalar function det: Mₙ(F) → F satisfying: multilinearity in rows, alternating (swap changes sign), det(I)=1. det(A)≠0 iff A invertible. Geometric meaning: signed volume of parallelepiped formed by rows.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.5; Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.10

concepts.name: Cofactor Expansion
concepts.description: det(A) = ∑_j a_{ij}C_{ij} along any row or column, where C_{ij}=(−1)^{i+j}M_{ij} is the cofactor and M_{ij} is the (i,j) minor. Efficient for small matrices; O(n!) complexity — not used for large n.

concepts.name: Properties of Determinants
concepts.description: Key properties: det(AB)=det(A)det(B), det(Aᵀ)=det(A), det(cA)=cⁿdet(A), det(A⁻¹)=1/det(A). Row operations: swap changes sign, scaling multiplies det by scalar, adding row multiples leaves det unchanged.

concepts.name: Cramer's Rule
concepts.description: For Ax=b with det(A)≠0, xᵢ = det(Aᵢ)/det(A), where Aᵢ replaces column i with b. Theoretically important (explicit formula), computationally impractical for large n.

concepts.name: LU Factorization
concepts.description: Decomposes A=LU (or PA=LU with pivoting), where L is lower triangular and U is upper triangular. Enables efficient solution of multiple systems with the same A via forward/back substitution.

concepts.name: Vector Space
concepts.description: A set V with vector addition and scalar multiplication satisfying 8 axioms (closure, commutativity, associativity, identity, inverses, scalar distributivity, vector distributivity, unit scalar). Examples: ℝⁿ, polynomials, function spaces.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.1

concepts.name: Subspace
concepts.description: A subset W of a vector space V that is itself a vector space under the inherited operations. Equivalent condition: W is closed under addition and scalar multiplication and contains the zero vector.

concepts.name: Span
concepts.description: span(S) = the set of all linear combinations of vectors in S. The smallest subspace containing S. A set spans V iff every vector in V can be expressed as a linear combination of vectors in S.

concepts.name: Linear Independence
concepts.description: Vectors v₁,…,vₖ are linearly independent iff c₁v₁+⋯+cₖvₖ=0 implies all cᵢ=0. Equivalently, no vector in the set is a linear combination of the others. Key property for basis construction.

concepts.name: Basis
concepts.description: A set of vectors that is both linearly independent and spans V. Every vector in V is uniquely expressible as a linear combination of basis vectors. The standard basis of ℝⁿ is {e₁,…,eₙ}.

concepts.name: Dimension
concepts.description: The number of vectors in any basis of V; all bases of V have the same cardinality. dim(ℝⁿ)=n. For subspaces: dim(U)+dim(U⊥)=dim(V).

concepts.name: Coordinates
concepts.description: Given an ordered basis β={b₁,…,bₙ}, the coordinate vector [v]_β = (c₁,…,cₙ) where v=∑cᵢbᵢ. Coordinates depend on the choice of basis. Change of basis converts between coordinate representations.

concepts.name: Change of Basis
concepts.description: If β and γ are bases, the change-of-basis matrix P_{β→γ} satisfies [v]_γ = P[v]_β. Matrix representations of a linear map in two bases are related by similarity: B = P⁻¹AP.

concepts.name: Null Space
concepts.description: N(A) = {x : Ax=0}; a subspace of ℝⁿ. Dimension is the nullity of A; its vectors are the solutions to the homogeneous system. Found by row-reducing A and parameterizing free variables.

concepts.name: Column Space
concepts.description: C(A) = {Ax : x∈ℝⁿ}; the span of the columns of A; a subspace of ℝᵐ. Ax=b is consistent iff b∈C(A). dim(C(A)) = rank(A).

concepts.name: Rank-Nullity Theorem
concepts.description: For a linear map T:V→W (or m×n matrix A): rank(T) + nullity(T) = dim(V) (= n for matrices). Constrains the sizes of image and kernel.

concepts.name: Linear Map
concepts.description: A map T:V→W satisfying T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v) and T(cv)=cT(v). Completely determined by its action on a basis. Every linear map between finite-dimensional spaces is represented by a matrix in given bases.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.3

concepts.name: Kernel and Image of Linear Map
concepts.description: ker(T) = {v : T(v)=0} is a subspace of V; im(T) = {T(v) : v∈V} is a subspace of W. T is injective iff ker(T)={0}; T is surjective iff im(T)=W.

concepts.name: Matrix Representation of Linear Map
concepts.description: Given ordered bases β of V and γ of W, the matrix [T]_{γβ} has columns [T(bⱼ)]_γ. Matrix multiplication then corresponds to composition of linear maps: [T∘S]=[T][S].

concepts.name: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
concepts.description: Av=λv for a nonzero vector v; λ is an eigenvalue, v is an eigenvector. Eigenvectors are fixed directions under the transformation; eigenvalues give the scaling factors.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.6; Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.5

concepts.name: Characteristic Polynomial
concepts.description: p(λ) = det(A−λI); eigenvalues are its roots. Degree n polynomial for n×n matrix. Cayley-Hamilton theorem states A satisfies its own characteristic polynomial: p(A)=0.

concepts.name: Eigenspace
concepts.description: E_λ = ker(A−λI) = {v : Av=λv}; a subspace of ℝⁿ. Its dimension is the geometric multiplicity of λ. Algebraic multiplicity ≥ geometric multiplicity; equality holds for diagonalizable matrices.

concepts.name: Diagonalization
concepts.description: A is diagonalizable iff it has n linearly independent eigenvectors. Then A=PDP⁻¹ where D=diag(λ₁,…,λₙ) and P has eigenvectors as columns. Powers: Aᵏ=PDᵏP⁻¹.

concepts.name: Matrix Exponential
concepts.description: e^A = ∑_{k=0}^∞ Aᵏ/k!. For diagonalizable A=PDP⁻¹: e^A = Pe^DP⁻¹. Used to express solutions to x′=Ax as x(t)=e^{At}x(0). Satisfies d/dt e^{At} = Ae^{At}.

concepts.name: Jordan Normal Form
concepts.description: Every matrix over ℂ is similar to a block-diagonal matrix with Jordan blocks Jₖ(λ) on the diagonal. Generalizes diagonalization to non-diagonalizable matrices. Jordan blocks have λ on the diagonal and 1s on the superdiagonal.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.8; Horn & Johnson, Matrix Analysis

concepts.name: Spectral Theorem
concepts.description: Every real symmetric (or complex Hermitian) matrix is orthogonally (unitarily) diagonalizable: A=QΛQᵀ with Q orthogonal and Λ real diagonal. Eigenvalues are real; eigenvectors from different eigenspaces are orthogonal.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.7

concepts.name: Positive Definite Matrix
concepts.description: A symmetric matrix A is positive definite iff vᵀAv>0 for all nonzero v, iff all eigenvalues are positive, iff all leading principal minors are positive (Sylvester's criterion). PSD: ≥0 instead of >0.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, §6.5; Horn & Johnson, Matrix Analysis

concepts.name: Cholesky Decomposition
concepts.description: A positive definite matrix A = LLᵀ where L is lower triangular with positive diagonal. More efficient than LU for symmetric positive definite systems (half the work). Widely used in numerical linear algebra.

concepts.name: Inner Product
concepts.description: A generalization of the dot product: a positive-definite symmetric bilinear form (real) or sesquilinear form (complex) ⟨·,·⟩:V×V→F. Induces a norm |v|=√⟨v,v⟩.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.6

concepts.name: Inner Product Space
concepts.description: A vector space equipped with an inner product. Cauchy-Schwarz inequality: |⟨u,v⟩| ≤ |u||v|. The norm makes it a normed space; if complete, it becomes a Hilbert space.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.6

concepts.name: Orthogonal and Orthonormal Basis
concepts.description: A basis where all vectors are mutually orthogonal (orthogonal basis) or additionally unit vectors (orthonormal basis). In an ONB, coordinates of v are simply ⟨v,eᵢ⟩. Expansion: v=∑⟨v,eᵢ⟩eᵢ.

concepts.name: Gram-Schmidt Process
concepts.description: Iteratively converts a linearly independent set into an orthonormal basis: subtract projections onto previous vectors, then normalize. Foundation of QR factorization.

concepts.name: Orthogonal Projection
concepts.description: The projection of v onto subspace W is the closest point in W to v, with v−proj_W(v) ⊥ W. For one vector: proj_u(v) = (v·u/u·u)u. For a subspace with ONB {e₁,…,eₖ}: proj = ∑⟨v,eᵢ⟩eᵢ.

concepts.name: Least Squares
concepts.description: When Ax=b has no solution, the least squares solution minimizes |Ax−b|². Found by the normal equations AᵀAx̂=Aᵀb. Solution: x̂=(AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀb when AᵀA is invertible.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.4

concepts.name: QR Factorization
concepts.description: A = QR where Q is orthogonal (Qᵀ=Q⁻¹) and R is upper triangular. Computed by Gram-Schmidt or Householder reflections. Used for least squares, eigenvalue computation (QR algorithm).
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, §4.4; Golub & Van Loan, Matrix Computations

concepts.name: Singular Value Decomposition
concepts.description: Any m×n matrix A = UΣVᵀ where U (m×m) and V (n×n) are orthogonal and Σ (m×n) is diagonal with non-negative entries (singular values). Most powerful matrix decomposition; foundation of numerical linear algebra and data science.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, §7.1; Golub & Van Loan, Matrix Computations, Ch.8

concepts.name: Singular Values
concepts.description: The singular values σᵢ = √(eigenvalues of AᵀA) = √(eigenvalues of AAᵀ). They generalize eigenvalues to non-square matrices; σ₁ = |A| (operator 2-norm). Encode the 'strength' of each mode of the transformation.

concepts.name: Moore-Penrose Pseudoinverse
concepts.description: A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ where Σ⁺ inverts each nonzero singular value and leaves zero singular values as zero. Gives the minimum-norm least squares solution x̂=A⁺b for any system Ax=b.

concepts.name: Tensor
concepts.description: A multilinear map T: V×⋯×V×V*×⋯×V*→F of type (r,s), where r contravariant and s covariant indices. Includes scalars (0,0), vectors (1,0), dual vectors (0,1), and matrices (1,1). Tensor product ⊗ constructs higher-rank tensors.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Appendix; Misner, Thorne & Wheeler, Gravitation, Ch.2 (tensors)

concepts.name: Dual Space
concepts.description: V* = Hom(V,F), the space of all linear functionals on V. dim(V*)=dim(V). Dual basis {f¹,…,fⁿ} satisfies fⁱ(eⱼ)=δⁱⱼ. Key in differential geometry (1-forms) and functional analysis.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.3

concepts.name: Distance in Vector Spaces
concepts.description: d(u,v) = |u−v|. Euclidean distance in ℝⁿ. The norm induces a metric satisfying non-negativity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.

concepts.name: Angle Between Vectors
concepts.description: cos θ = (a·b)/(|a||b|). θ=π/2 iff a⊥b. Cauchy-Schwarz inequality guarantees this value is in [−1,1], ensuring θ is well-defined.
